subject: Criminal Law
lesson_title: Burglary (s.12 Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001)

questions.question: Section 12 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 defines burglary. Which of the following correctly states the elements of burglary?

options.A: A person enters any building and steals property therein
options.B: A person enters any building or part of a building as a trespasser with intent to commit a theft offence, assault causing harm or greater, or criminal damage therein; or having entered as a trespasser, commits theft, assault causing harm or greater, or criminal damage therein
options.C: A person breaks into any building by force with intent to steal property belonging to another
options.D: A person enters a dwelling house at night with intent to commit any felony therein

questions.explanation: Section 12 CJ(T&F)A 2001 defines burglary with two limbs: (1) Entry limb — entering any building or part of a building as a trespasser with intent to commit a s.12 offence (theft, assault causing harm or greater, or criminal damage); or (2) Conduct limb — having entered as a trespasser, actually committing theft, assault causing harm or greater, or criminal damage. Key elements: (a) 'entry' — the nature of which is governed by case law (R v Collins, R v Brown, R v Ryan); (b) 'as a trespasser' — without permission to enter; (c) 'building or part of a building' — including parts to which access is restricted; (d) the specified ulterior offences. Option A omits the trespass element and requires actual theft. Option C introduces a 'breaking' requirement that does not appear in the Irish Act. Option D uses the antiquated common law formulation (breaking and entering a dwelling at night) which has been replaced by the 2001 Act.

questions.question: In R v Collins [1972] CA (England and Wales), the accused was a young man who, having climbed a ladder and seen a naked woman asleep through a window, undressed and climbed onto the window sill. The woman woke up and, mistaking him for her boyfriend, invited him in. They had sexual intercourse. Collins was charged with burglary. The central issue was whether he had entered 'as a trespasser.' What principle did the court establish regarding entry?

options.A: Any physical crossing of the threshold of a building constitutes entry sufficient for burglary — there is no requirement that the entry be substantial
options.B: For the purposes of burglary, entry must be 'effective and substantial' — merely having part of the body technically within the threshold may not constitute entry if the entry is not effective and substantial; additionally, the accused must enter as a trespasser (knowing they are, or reckless as to whether they are, entering without permission)
options.C: Entry requires forced breaking of the threshold — entry through an open window or door without force is insufficient
options.D: Entry is complete the moment any body part crosses the threshold, regardless of whether the accused was aware they were trespassing

questions.explanation: R v Collins [1972] CA established two critical principles for burglary: (1) Entry must be 'effective and substantial' — a minimal technical intrusion of a body part across a threshold may not constitute the entry required for burglary; and (2) The accused must enter as a trespasser — knowing they are trespassing (i.e., without permission) or being reckless as to whether they have permission. In Collins, the accused was acquitted because there was a factual question about whether the woman's invitation (albeit mistaken) meant he was not entering as a trespasser — he may have believed she was inviting him in knowing who he was. The 'effective and substantial' test has been influential in subsequent cases. Collins remains the leading authority on the mens rea element of trespass in burglary. Option A incorrectly removes any substantiality requirement. Option C invents a force requirement. Option D removes the mens rea for trespass.

questions.question: In R v Brown [1985] CA (England and Wales), the accused was found with his upper body inside a broken shop window, with his legs remaining outside on the pavement, rummaging inside. Was this sufficient 'entry' for burglary?

options.A: No — because the accused's feet had not crossed the threshold, no complete entry had occurred
options.B: Yes — the insertion of the upper body into the building was sufficient entry; entry does not require the entire body to pass the threshold; the entry was effective in that the accused could reach inside to steal
options.C: No — entry through a broken window (as opposed to a door) cannot constitute entry for the purposes of burglary
options.D: Yes — but only because the accused was found in the act of stealing; without the theft, the partial entry would be insufficient

questions.explanation: R v Brown [1985] CA established that entry for burglary does not require the entire body to pass the threshold. The accused's insertion of his upper body through the broken shop window was sufficient entry. The court applied an 'effective' test — the entry was effective because the accused could reach inside and access the shop's contents for the purpose of stealing. This case developed the Collins 'effective and substantial' test: the entry was effective (enabling access for the criminal purpose) even though the accused's legs remained outside. Brown is commonly tested alongside Collins and Ryan. Option A incorrectly requires complete bodily entry. Option C invents a restriction based on the mode of entry (window vs. door). Option D incorrectly conditions the sufficiency of entry on whether theft was actually completed.

questions.question: In R v Ryan [1996] CA (England and Wales), Ryan was found stuck in a window of a house with his head and right arm inside and the rest of his body outside. He was freed by the fire brigade. Was this sufficient entry for burglary?

options.A: No — Ryan's entry was incomplete and involuntary (he was stuck); an involuntary entry cannot constitute the voluntary entry required for burglary
options.B: Yes — the court held that entry was established; it was not necessary that the entry be effective in enabling the accused to commit the ulterior offence; the partial entry (head and arm inside) constituted entry for the purposes of burglary even though Ryan was physically incapacitated
options.C: No — because Ryan did not have any intent to steal at the time of the partial entry; intent must be contemporaneous with effective entry
options.D: Yes — but only because Ryan's head had crossed the threshold; if only his arm had been inside, entry would be insufficient

questions.explanation: R v Ryan [1996] CA is the most extreme English case on the entry requirement for burglary. The court upheld Ryan's conviction for burglary despite his being physically stuck with only his head and right arm inside the building. The case moved away from the requirement that the entry be 'effective' (in the sense of enabling commission of the ulterior offence) — Ryan clearly could not steal anything in his trapped state. The court held that entry was established by the partial intrusion. This case is sometimes criticised for its broad approach (if someone's arm is stuck in a window, can they really have 'entered'?) but it remains the authority. The case trilogy Collins — Brown — Ryan shows a progression in how loosely courts have interpreted 'entry.' Option A introduces an involuntariness argument that was rejected. Option C incorrectly adds a contemporaneity of intent requirement. Option D creates a hierarchy of body parts that has no basis in the case law.

questions.question: In R v Baker [1994] CA (England and Wales), Baker was a customer in a shop who entered the publicly accessible part of the shop and then went behind the staff counter — an area clearly not open to customers. Was Baker a trespasser in the staff area?

options.A: No — Baker had permission to enter the building; having permission to enter any part of a building means he was not a trespasser in any part of it
options.B: Yes — Baker's permission to enter extended only to the customer area; by entering the staff area, he exceeded the scope of his permission and became a trespasser in that part; burglary can be committed by entering a restricted part of a building as a trespasser
options.C: No — trespass requires a prohibition communicated by a legal notice; an informal understanding that the staff area is restricted is insufficient to make entry trespassory
options.D: Yes — but only if the staff area was physically marked off with a barrier; without a physical barrier, the area cannot be a separate 'part of a building' for burglary purposes

questions.explanation: R v Baker [1994] CA established that where a person has permission to enter part of a building but enters a different part to which their permission does not extend, they enter that restricted part as a trespasser. Baker was permitted to enter the customer area of the shop but not the staff area. By going behind the counter, he exceeded the scope of his permission and became a trespasser in that part of the building. Section 12 CJ(T&F)A 2001 expressly includes 'part of a building' — consistent with the English position. This principle prevents a person from using general permission to enter a building as a shield against burglary charges when they penetrate into restricted areas. Option A incorrectly treats general permission to enter as blanket permission for all parts. Option C creates a formal notice requirement that does not exist. Option D adds a physical barrier requirement that was not imposed in Baker.

questions.question: What is the maximum sentence for burglary under s.12 CJ(T&F)A 2001 on conviction on indictment?
questions.explanation: The maximum sentence for burglary under s.12 CJ(T&F)A 2001 on conviction on indictment is 14 years imprisonment. This is significantly higher than theft (10 years) and reflects the additional element of trespass in a building — the privacy and security of premises is treated as an aggravating factor warranting greater punishment than mere theft. The elevated maximum also reflects the potential for violence or confrontation inherent in burglary scenarios. The even higher penalties for aggravated burglary (life imprisonment under s.13) reflect the further escalation where the accused carries weapons. Options A, B, and D state incorrect maximum sentences — life imprisonment is the penalty for aggravated burglary, not burglary.

questions.question: Kevin is invited to a house party by his friend Sarah. While at the party, Kevin goes upstairs — an area the host had told guests was off-limits — and steals jewellery from the host's bedroom. Has Kevin committed burglary?

options.A: No — Kevin had permission to enter the house; having general permission to enter means he cannot be a trespasser for burglary purposes
options.B: Yes — Kevin's permission extended to the downstairs area (the party area); by going upstairs to the off-limits area, he exceeded the scope of his permission and entered the bedroom as a trespasser; having entered as a trespasser, he committed theft therein — satisfying the conduct limb of s.12 burglary
options.C: No — Kevin was at the party legally; the host's request that guests not go upstairs is a social convention, not a legal prohibition creating trespass
options.D: Yes — but only on the basis that Kevin formed the intent to steal before entering the house, not because of what occurred after entry

questions.explanation: Applying R v Baker [1994] CA: Kevin had permission to enter the house for the party, but that permission was limited to the downstairs area. The host expressly told guests not to go upstairs — making clear that Kevin's permission did not extend to upstairs areas. By entering the upstairs bedroom, Kevin exceeded the scope of his permission and entered that part of the building as a trespasser. Having entered the bedroom as a trespasser, he committed theft therein. This satisfies the conduct limb of s.12 CJ(T&F)A 2001 burglary: a person who enters any part of a building as a trespasser and commits theft therein. The maximum sentence of 14 years applies. Option A incorrectly treats general entry permission as extending to all parts. Option C treats the host's prohibition as a mere social convention — legally, exceeding the scope of permission constitutes trespass. Option D narrowly focuses on pre-entry intent when the conduct limb of s.12 is equally applicable.

questions.question: Which of the following ulterior offences, if intended at the time of trespassory entry, can found a burglary charge under s.12 CJ(T&F)A 2001?

options.A: Any criminal offence — if a person enters as a trespasser intending to commit any crime inside, they commit burglary
options.B: Theft, assault causing harm or greater (including serious harm), or criminal damage — these are the specified ulterior offences under s.12; entry with intent to commit other offences does not satisfy the entry limb of burglary
options.C: Theft or robbery only — the ulterior intent must relate to property offences
options.D: Murder, rape, or theft — these are the three serious offences specified in s.12

questions.explanation: Section 12 CJ(T&F)A 2001 specifies that for the entry limb of burglary, the accused must enter as a trespasser with intent to commit one of the following: (1) a theft offence under the 2001 Act; (2) an assault causing harm or greater (under the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997); or (3) criminal damage. Entry with intent to commit other offences (for example, entry with intent to commit rape or drug offences) does not constitute burglary under s.12, though it may give rise to other charges. For the conduct limb, the accused must actually commit theft, assault causing harm or greater, or criminal damage having entered as a trespasser. This specification of ulterior offences reflects the legislative policy of targeting those who use buildings as venues for property and violence offences. Option A incorrectly extends the ulterior intent to any crime. Option C incorrectly limits it to property offences only. Option D includes murder and rape which are not specified.

questions.question: Marta is employed as a cleaner at an office block and has a key to enter early in the morning. One day she enters at her usual time with the intention of stealing office equipment. Has she entered as a trespasser?

options.A: No — Marta has a key and authorised access to the building; she cannot be a trespasser regardless of her ulterior intent
options.B: Yes — although Marta has physical access through her key, her permission to enter is for the purpose of cleaning; where she enters with a fundamentally different purpose (theft) that goes beyond the scope of her permission, she enters as a trespasser; permission can be exceeded by the purpose for which it is used
options.C: No — a person with a physical key to a building can never be a trespasser in that building, regardless of their intent upon entry
options.D: Yes — but only if she formed the intent to steal after entering; if she formed the intent before using the key, she is not a trespasser because she used her own key

questions.explanation: This scenario illustrates the principle that permission to enter a building is typically granted for a specific purpose, and entering for a fundamentally different — especially criminal — purpose can constitute trespassory entry. Marta's permission to enter the office block is granted for the purpose of cleaning. When she enters with the intention of stealing equipment, she enters for a purpose that is wholly outside and antithetical to the scope of her permission. While the position is more nuanced than a simple 'key = authorised' analysis, the principle from R v Collins and subsequent cases is that entering as a trespasser refers to entering without permission, or in excess of permission. Courts have held that entering property to commit theft is outside the scope of any permission granted for a legitimate purpose. This extends Baker's part-of-building principle to permission limited by purpose. Option A treats physical access as absolute permission. Option C removes trespass liability entirely for key-holders. Option D inverts the correct analysis — forming the intent before entry is the classic entry limb of burglary (intent contemporaneous with entry).
